JavaScript Core Concepts (15): Operators Are Basically Tiny Functions
Next up: operators. You use these constantly, no matter what you're building.
An operator is really just a simple function that JavaScript hands you. Take the equals sign in an assignment — that's the assignment operator:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
The values on either side are called "operands". The number of operands is what makes something a unary, binary, or ternary operator, but most of the time you're dealing with binary ones.
Basically every operator is an expression, because it always returns a result:
If you check the MDN docs, there's a whole pile of them:
One thing worth pointing out here: the
we've been using a lot is also an operator — a unary one, in fact. Most unary operators start with a keyword.
Last thing, and this one's a bit of a curiosity. I just said operators are basically simple functions. So what if you wrote something like this?
That doesn't actually throw an error, because an operator really is just a simple function under the hood. The mental model is something like:

It won't give you the "correct" result though — it just grabs the last value. This is purely to illustrate the concept. And honestly, even if that syntax worked, writing operators like a function call would be a pain in real code. So instead we write
, with the plus sign sitting in the middle. There's a name for that style: an "infix expression" — meaning you put the function name between its two arguments.
So an operator placed in front is a prefix expression, and one placed after is a postfix expression.

Next up: operators. You use them constantly no matter what you're building. This post covers what an operator actually is, why every operator is really just a tiny function under the hood, the operand counts (unary, binary, ternary), and what infix, prefix, and postfix expressions mean.
